original_abstract: While catalytic borylations of sp³ C–H bonds typically favor primary positions or electronically activated secondary sites, functionalization at tertiary C–H bonds has remained an elusive challenge. In this work, we report a versatile iridium-catalyzed method for the bridgehead borylation of bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes and (hetero)bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes. This transformation is highly site-selective for the tertiary C–H bond and demonstrates broad functional group tolerance across more than 35 examples. We show that the protocol is effective for both the late-stage diversification of complex pharmaceuticals and the streamlined synthesis of novel bicyclic building blocks. Mechanistic investigations, including kinetic analysis and computational modeling, indicate that C–H bond cleavage occurs with a relatively low energy barrier. Instead, the turnover-limiting step is identified as an isomerization process required prior to the C–B bond-forming reductive elimination.
humanized_abstract: Catalytic borylation of sp3 C–H bonds generally favors the primary positions or preactivated secondary sites. So far, borylation of tertiary C–H bonds has not been realized. Here, we describe an iridium catalytic method for the selective bridgehead borylation of bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes and (hetero)bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes with excellent site selectivity for the tertiary C–H bond and high functional group compatibility that accommodates over 35 different substrates. The methodology allows not only late stage diversification of several biologically important pharmaceuticals but also the rapid assembly of many diverse bicyclic cores. Kinetic analysis and theoretical studies suggested that the C–H activation of tertiary C–H bonds occurs with moderate barrier. However, the turnover limiting step was found to be an isomerization step prior to the reduction elimination to form the C–B bond.

The methodology allows not only late stage diversification of several biologically important pharmaceuticals but also the rapid assembly of many diverse bicyclic cores. Kinetic analysis and theoretical studies suggested that the C–H activation of tertiary C–H bonds occurs with moderate barrier. However, the turnover limiting step was found to be an isomerization step prior to the reduction elimination to form the C–B bond.
